en.extract:
Humblot's heron (Ardea humbloti), also known as the Madagascar heron, is a species of heron. It is commonly found on the north and west coasts of Madagascar but it is also present in the Comoro Islands and Mayotte. Humblot's heron is considered an endangered species due to its declining population. The major threats the heron faces are poaching, habitat destruction, and the invasion of alien species such as the water hyacinth.


Taxonomy
Humblot's heron is part of the subfamily Ardeinae, which are known as "typical herons". It has been suggested that Humblot's heron may be a darker morph of the grey heron due to their many similar physical characteristics, but this claim has been successfully refuted. Humblot's heron was formally described by Henri Milne-Edwards and Alfred Grandidier in 1885.
Local Malagasy names for Humblot's heron include "Kisirano", "Langaro" and "Langaroko", however these names are also associated with different species of herons, such as the grey heron.
The scientific name commemorates the French naturalist Leon Humblot.


Description
Humblot's heron has a uniform dark gray plumage on its body and darker feathers on its forehead and chin. Its nape feathers are elongated to form a crest. It has a large, pale bill as well as pale eyes and legs.
Humblot's heron is 92–100 cm (3.02–3.28 ft) tall. Its body weight can range between 1–1.6 kg (2.2–3.5 lb). The bill length ranges from 13–14.93 cm (5.12–5.88 in) while the bill depth ranges from 2.45–2.69 cm (0.96–1.06 in).


Distribution and habitat
Humblot's heron is endemic to the island of Madagascar and is most commonly found in the coastal lowlands of southern, western and northern Madagascar. Small populations have also been found in the neighboring islands of Comoros and Mayotte. There has been one recorded and accepted observation of the heron in mainland Africa, in a game reserve in Tanzania. The total population of Humblot's heron in Madagascar was estimated in 2006 at 1,000 to 3,000 individuals.

Humblot's heron inhabits fresh, brackish or saltwater wetlands. It prefers tidal areas but also inhabits lakes, rivers, mangroves and estuaries. It is found most commonly at low elevations but has been recorded up to an altitude of 1,500 m (4,900 ft). It does not migrate, however it is able to disperse over long distances, as shown by its presence in mainland Africa.


Behavior


Diet
Humblot's heron primarily eats medium and large-sized fish as well as crustaceans. They forage on floating vegetation in clear, shallow water or in the ingoing and outgoing tides. They are passive feeders, therefore they remain stationary for long periods of time, often hunched or with their neck extended over the water. After catching their prey, they will fly to solid ground to consume it.


Reproduction
Little information is known about its breeding cycle. Nesting has been observed year-round. Nests are typically on the ground and surrounded by dense aquatic vegetation. They can also be found in trees between 5–12 m (16–39 ft) tall. In Mayotte, the heron was observed nesting in mangroves.
The Humblot's heron's nest is mostly made of sticks and reeds and is approximately 50–70 cm (20–28 in) in diameter. Their eggs are blue and the typical clutch size is 3 eggs. Both parents take turns incubating the eggs.


Vocalizations
Its vocalizations are similar to the vocalizations of the grey heron. It is an irregular barking sound that lasts 0.5-1.0 s. Humblot's heron is also known to rattle its bill quietly while in its nest.


Conservation status
Humblot's heron is considered endangered, per the IUCN Red List. Its population is declining most likely due to poaching, both of the eggs and of the bird. Additionally, due to the increasing human population in Western Madagascar, the wetlands where the heron nests are being transformed more and more into rice paddies. The increasingly drier climate is also contributing to the degradation of their preferred nesting habitats. The rate at which its population is decreasing has not yet been established.


References


External links
BirdLife Species Factsheet.
Range map of Ardea humbloti
Audio recordings of Ardea humbloti vocalizations

'''Humblot's heron''' ('''''Ardea humbloti'''''), also known as the '''Madagascar heron''', is a [[species]] of [[heron]]. It is commonly found on the north and west coasts of [[Madagascar]] but it is also present in the [[Comoro Islands]] and [[Mayotte]]. Humblot's heron is considered an [[endangered species]] due to its declining population. The major threats the heron faces are [[poaching]], [[habitat destruction]], and the invasion of alien species such as the [[water hyacinth]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Weseloh |first1=D. V. Chip |last2=Green |first2=M. Clay |date=2018-12-01 |title=Herons of the World Symposium: Their Biology and Conservation - an Introduction |journal=Waterbirds |volume=41 |issue=4 |page=341 |doi=10.1675/063.041.0410 |issn=1524-4695}}</ref>

== Taxonomy ==
Humblot's heron is part of the subfamily [[Ardeinae]], which are known as "typical herons".<ref>{{Cite web |title=HeronConservation » Heron Taxonomy and Evolution |url=https://www.heronconservation.org/herons-of-the-world/heron-taxonomy-and-evolution/ |access-date=2023-10-18 |language=en-US}}</ref> It has been suggested that Humblot's heron may be a darker morph of the [[grey heron]] due to their many similar physical characteristics, but this claim has been successfully refuted.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Behrens |first=Kenneth D. |date=2021 |title=Humblot's Heron (Ardea humbloti), version 2.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/humher1/cur/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.humher1.02 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Humblot's heron was formally described by [[Henri Milne-Edwards]] and [[Alfred Grandidier]] in 1885.

Local Malagasy names for Humblot's heron include "Kisirano", "Langaro" and "Langaroko", however these names are also associated with different species of herons, such as the grey heron.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Gardner |first1=Charlie J. |last2=Andriamahenina |first2=Zo |last3=Carro |first3=Aude |last4=Jones |first4=Trevor G. |last5=Jasper |first5=Louise D. |date=2017-02-01 |title=Rapid assessments and local knowledge reveal high bird diversity in mangroves of north-west Madagascar |journal=Wetlands Ecology and Management |language=en |volume=25 |issue=1 |pages=45–58 |doi=10.1007/s11273-016-9501-3 |bibcode=2017WetEM..25...45G |issn=1572-9834}}</ref>

The scientific name commemorates the [[France|French]] naturalist [[Leon Humblot]].<ref>{{cite book|author=Jobling, James A.|title=''Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names''|url=https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling|year=2010 |publisher=A&C Black | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 }}</ref>

== Description ==
Humblot's heron has a uniform dark gray plumage on its body and darker feathers on its forehead and chin. Its nape feathers are elongated to form a crest. It has a large, pale bill as well as pale eyes and legs.<ref name=":0" />

Humblot's heron is {{Convert|92-100|cm|ft|abbr=on}} tall.<ref name=":0" /> Its body weight can range between {{Convert|1-1.6|kg|lbs|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Bird |first1=Jeremy P. |last2=Martin |first2=Robert |last3=Akçakaya |first3=H. Reşit |last4=Gilroy |first4=James |last5=Burfield |first5=Ian J. |last6=Garnett |first6=Stephen T. |last7=Symes |first7=Andy |last8=Taylor |first8=Joseph |last9=Şekercioğlu |first9=Çağan H. |last10=Butchart |first10=Stuart H. M. |date=October 2020 |title=Generation lengths of the world's birds and their implications for extinction risk |url=https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/cobi.13486 |journal=Conservation Biology |language=en |volume=34 |issue=5 |pages=1252–1261 |doi=10.1111/cobi.13486 |pmid=32058610 |bibcode=2020ConBi..34.1252B |issn=0888-8892}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last1=Tobias |first1=Joseph A. |last2=Sheard |first2=Catherine |last3=Pigot |first3=Alex L. |last4=Devenish |first4=Adam J. A. |last13=Jones |first13=Samuel E. I. |last14=Vincent |first14=Claire |last15=Phillips |first15=Anna G. |date=March 2022 |editor-last=Coulson |editor-first=Tim |title=AVONET: morphological, ecological and geographical data for all birds |journal=Ecology Letters |language=en |volume=25 |issue=3 |pages=581–597 |doi=10.1111/ele.13898 |issn=1461-023X|doi-access=free |pmid=35199922 |bibcode=2022EcolL..25..581T |hdl=1893/34052 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> The bill length ranges from {{Convert|13-14.93|cm|in|abbr=on}} while the bill depth ranges from {{Convert|2.45-2.69|cm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":1" />

== Distribution and habitat ==
Humblot's heron is endemic to the island of Madagascar and is most commonly found in the coastal lowlands of southern, western and northern Madagascar. Small populations have also been found in the neighboring islands of Comoros and Mayotte. There has been one recorded and accepted observation of the heron in mainland Africa, in a game reserve in Tanzania.<ref name=":0" /> The total population of Humblot's heron in Madagascar was estimated in 2006 at 1,000 to 3,000 individuals.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Young |first1=H. Glyn |last2=Young |first2=R. P. |last3=Razafindrajao |first3=F. |last4=Aboudou |first4=Abdallah Iahia Bin |last5=Fa |first5=J. E. |date=2014 |title=Patterns of waterbird diversity in central western Madagascar: where are the priority sites for conservation? |url=https://wildfowl.wwt.org.uk/index.php/wildfowl/article/view/2580 |journal=Wildfowl |language=en |volume=64 |issue=64 |page=35 |issn=2052-6458}}</ref>
[[File:Ardea humbloti map.svg|thumb|225x225px|Range of ''A. humbloti'']]
Humblot's heron inhabits fresh, brackish or saltwater wetlands. It prefers tidal areas but also inhabits lakes, rivers, mangroves and estuaries. It is found most commonly at low elevations but has been recorded up to an altitude of {{Convert|1500|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":0" /> It does not migrate, however it is able to disperse over long distances, as shown by its presence in mainland Africa.<ref name=":0" />

== Behavior ==

=== Diet ===
Humblot's heron primarily eats medium and large-sized fish as well as crustaceans. They forage on floating vegetation in clear, shallow water or in the ingoing and outgoing tides. They are passive feeders, therefore they remain stationary for long periods of time, often hunched or with their neck extended over the water. After catching their prey, they will fly to solid ground to consume it.<ref name=":0" />

=== Reproduction ===
Little information is known about its breeding cycle. Nesting has been observed year-round. Nests are typically on the ground and surrounded by dense aquatic vegetation. They can also be found in trees between {{Convert|5-12|m|ft|abbr=on}} tall. In Mayotte, the heron was observed nesting in mangroves.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Rabarisoa |first1=Rivo |last2=Ramanampamonjy |first2=Julien |last3=Razafindrajao |first3=Felix |last4=Roland |first4=Lily-Arison Rene De |last5=Jeanne |first5=François |last6=Bacar |first6=Ousseni |last7=Laubin |first7=Alexandre |last8=Bignon |first8=Florent |date=May 2020 |title=Status Assessment and Population Trends of the Madagascar Pond-Heron (Ardeola idae) from 1993-2016 |url=https://bioone.org/journals/waterbirds/volume-43/issue-1/063.043.0105/Status-Assessment-and-Population-Trends-of-the-Madagascar-Pond-Heron/10.1675/063.043.0105.full |journal=Waterbirds |volume=43 |issue=1 |pages=45–54 |doi=10.1675/063.043.0105 |issn=1524-4695|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

The Humblot's heron's nest is mostly made of sticks and reeds and is approximately {{Convert|50-70|cm|in|abbr=on}} in diameter. Their eggs are blue and the typical clutch size is 3 eggs. Both parents take turns incubating the eggs.<ref name=":0" />

=== Vocalizations ===
Its vocalizations are similar to the vocalizations of the grey heron. It is an irregular barking sound that lasts 0.5-1.0 s. Humblot's heron is also known to rattle its bill quietly while in its nest.<ref name=":0" />[[File:Humblot's Heron - Ankarafantsika - Madagascar (15294585941).jpg|thumb|Humblot's heron in [[Ankarafantsika National Park]], Madagascar|left]]

== Conservation status ==
Humblot's heron is considered endangered, per the [[IUCN Red List]]. Its population is declining most likely due to poaching, both of the eggs and of the bird. Additionally, due to the increasing human population in Western Madagascar, the wetlands where the heron nests are being transformed more and more into [[rice paddies]]. The increasingly drier climate is also contributing to the degradation of their preferred nesting habitats. The rate at which its population is decreasing has not yet been established.<ref>{{Cite report |url=http://www.iucnredlist.org/details/22697012/0 |title=Ardea humbloti: BirdLife International: The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species 2016: e.T22697012A93598644 |last=IUCN |date=2016-10-01 |publisher=International Union for Conservation of Nature |doi=10.2305/iucn.uk.2016-3.rlts.t22697012a93598644.en |language=en|doi-access=free }}</ref>
